summary: This notebook demonstrates a regression-to-classification pipeline for the Child Mind Institute competition, focusing on handling mixed tabular and time-series data. It extracts statistical features from time-series parquets via a custom PyTorch autoencoder and creates domain-specific interaction features. The author trains multiple gradient boosting and neural network models using 5-fold stratified cross-validation, optimizes prediction thresholds on out-of-fold scores to maximize quadratic weighted kappa, and combines results through multiple voting regressors and final majority voting. Readers should learn how to bridge time-series feature extraction with tabular modeling, apply threshold tuning for ordinal regression metrics, and structure robust ensembling strategies.
competition_flow: Loads tabular and time-series data, extracts autoencoder-derived and hand-crafted features, trains LightGBM, XGBoost, CatBoost, and TabNet via 5-fold stratified CV, optimizes prediction thresholds for QWK, and generates a final submission via majority voting across three ensemble configurations.
data_reading: CSV files for main data and sample submission
Parquet files for time-series data loaded via pd.read_parquet within a ThreadPoolExecutor
data_processing: Manual label encoding for categorical Season columns
Dropping columns with excessive missing values
features_engineering: Hand-crafted physiological and behavioral interaction features (e.g., BMI_Age, Hydration_Status, Muscle_to_Fat)
Manual label encoding for categorical season indicators
ensembling: Combines predictions from three distinct VotingRegressor configurations (weighted and unweighted) and applies a final majority vote across submissions; optimizes prediction thresholds on out-of-fold scores using Nelder-Mead to maximize QWK before rounding.
insights: Autoencoder-derived features effectively capture time-series patterns for tabular models.
Threshold optimization on out-of-fold predictions significantly improves ordinal regression metrics compared to naive rounding.
Majority voting across diverse ensemble configurations stabilizes predictions and mitigates individual model bias.
